--

How a fan argument turned into a cross-platform app — and what I learned about shipping native mobile with a web stack
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Every basketball debate eventually hits the same wall: someone pulls out a number, someone disputes the source, and nobody has a clean way to compare two players side by side right now, in this conversation.
That friction is what started “My Player Compare” — a cross-platform iOS and Android app built entirely with web technologies: React 19, Vite, Capacitor 8, and Recharts. No Swift. No Kotlin. One codebase, two stores.
🏀 Download the app on App Store and Google Play.
Here’s how it’s built, and what actually worked.
The Tech Stack
To ensure a premium UX, high frame rates for charts, and absolute efficiency during build pipelines, I selected the following stack:

• React 19 & Vite: Provides lightning-fast compilation, HMR (Hot Module Replacement), and a reactive UI engine.

• Capacitor 8: A lightweight web-to-native wrapper that exposes native APIs (Share, Filesystem, and HTTP stack) via standard plugins, offering a high-performance alternative to React Native.

• Recharts: A declarative React chart library used to build radar charts, line trends, and donut charts.

• HTML2Canvas: A utility to convert HTML nodes into raw canvas buffers for easy image exports.

• Custom CSS (Variables & Dark Mode): A custom styling system built from scratch with HSL colors and sleek glassmorphism animations, avoiding the overhead of heavy UI frameworks.
Why Capacitor over React Native
React Native is the obvious choice — until you look at bundle complexity and the native module ecosystem for a data-heavy app. Capacitor takes a different approach: your app is a real web view, and native APIs (filesystem, share sheet, HTTP) are exposed through lightweight plugins. The result is predictable performance, straightforward debugging in a browser, and a build pipeline that frontend developers already know.
For a charts-heavy app where the UI is mostly declarative React, Capacitor was the right call.
Key Features & Under-the-Hood Implementations
1. Recent Form & Consistency Rating
Instead of just rendering season averages, our app analyzes the variance of a player’s last 5 games. I map the statistical spread (Min, Avg, Max) to calculate a Consistency Rating:

• If the variance is under 40% of the season average, the player is marked “Very Consistent”.

• If the variance is under 80%, it is marked “Moderate”.

• Otherwise, the player is flagged with “High Variance”.
Here is the calculation logic and range-bar rendering setup:
const rawVals = (p.recentGamesRaw?.[statKey] || []).map(v => parseFloat(v) || 0);
const minVal = Math.min(...rawVals);
const maxVal = Math.max(...rawVals);
const avgVal = rawVals.reduce((a, b) => a + b, 0) / rawVals.length;// Stability/Consistency calculation:
const spread = maxVal - minVal;
let stabilityLabel = '';
let stabilityColor = '';
if (spread < avgVal * 0.4) {
stabilityLabel = 'Very Consistent';
stabilityColor = '#00ea8c';
} else if (spread < avgVal * 0.8) {
stabilityLabel = 'Moderate';
stabilityColor = '#FFD700';
} else {
stabilityLabel = 'High Variance';
stabilityColor = '#FF4D4D';
}
In the UI, this translates to a custom range bar where a diamond icon marks the season average, and faded dots map each of the last 5 games, making hot streaks and slumps instantly visible.
2. Scoring Mix Visualization
To help users understand a player’s offensive profile (are they a paint-dominant 2PT player, a 3PT specialist, or a free-throw drawer?), I implemented a Recharts donut chart detailing the percentage of points scored by type:
<PieChart width={150} height={150}>
<Pie
data={mixData}
cx={75} cy={75}
innerRadius={45} outerRadius={70}
paddingAngle={2}
dataKey="value"
>
{mixData.map((entry, idx) => (
<Cell key={idx} fill={entry.color} stroke="transparent" />
))}
</Pie>
<text x={75} y={70} textAnchor="middle" fill="var(--text-main)" fontSize={16} fontWeight={700}>
{totalMixPts.toFixed(1)}
</text>
<text x={75} y={88} textAnchor="middle" fill="var(--text-muted)" fontSize={10}>
pts/g
</text>
</PieChart>3. Metric Comparison Matrix & Efficiency Calculations
Beyond basic per-game columns, I compute:

• Basic Efficiency: A standard formula accounting for positive contributions minus misses.

• Game Score (GmSC): John Hollinger’s simplified metric to determine productivity for a single game or season.

• Fantasy Points: Standard fantasy basketball points based on blocks, steals, points, and turnovers.
4. Graphic Poster Generation & Native Sharing
Users wanted to share comparisons directly to Twitter or Instagram. The solution: an off-screen DOM container rendered to canvas via html2canvas, then handed to Capacitor's native share plugin. On desktop it falls back to a direct PNG download.

The tricky part was retina scaling — scale: 2 in the html2canvas options was the only change needed for crisp output on modern screens.
Technical Challenges & Workarounds
1. Bypassing CORS: Web Dev vs. Native Mobile
During development, direct requests to third-party sports APIs are typically blocked by browser CORS security policies. I bypassed this during local development by configuring a development proxy inside vite.config.js.
However, in the native production app, configuration proxies are unavailable. To solve this, Capacitor automatically translates standard web fetch requests into native HTTP operations via Java/Swift when using @capacitor-community/http (or built-in CapacitorHttp). This bypasses browser sandbox limitations entirely, eliminating CORS issues in production without requiring an intermediate proxy server.
2. Offline-First Caching
Sports metrics update daily, but mobile users frequently travel and lose internet connections. I implemented a hybrid sync scheme:

• Local player data is saved using a LocalStorage adapter.

• The data is assigned a 4-hour stale threshold.

• Upon startup, the app silently syncs stale players in the background. If the user is offline, they can still view their saved rosters and compare statistics seamlessly.
Future Roadmap

• Multi-Sport Support: Partially implemented data models for Football, Soccer, Hockey and Baseball stats will be fully integrated.

• Compare 3+ Players: Expanding the UI grids to support comparing larger matrices of players on tablet-sized displays.

• Historical Data Comparison: Allowing comparisons of a player’s current campaign to historical seasons to chart career growth or decline.

• Advanced Predictive Analytics: Integrating a lightweight machine learning model (or integrating a sports data AI service) to forecast a player’s performance in upcoming matchups based on historical data against specific defensive schemes.

• Advanced Visual Analytics (Radar & Heatmaps): Utilizing Recharts to its full potential by adding interactive 2D court heatmaps that show shot distribution (zones, volume, and efficiency) alongside standard statistical trends.
The takeaway
A modern web stack — React, Vite, Capacitor — can ship a genuinely native-feeling mobile app with charts, file system access, and native sharing, without touching platform-specific code. The gap between “web app” and “mobile app” is smaller than it used to be.
🏀 My Player Compare is available on the App Store and Google Play.
